  C16 Migration Decision Record — ADR-2026-001 v1.1 :root { --c16-purple: #6B3FA0; --c16-pink: #B5567A; --c16-gold: #C97B3A; --c16-dark: #2c3e50; --c16-green: #27ae60; --c16-red: #e74c3c; --c16-orange: #e67e22; --c16-blue: #3498db; } \* { box-sizing: border-box; margin: 0; padding: 0; } body { font-family: 'Segoe UI', Arial, Helvetica, sans-serif; color: var(--c16-dark); background: #e9ecef; line-height: 1.6; } .doc-wrapper { max-width: 1120px; margin: 20px auto; background: #fff; box-shadow: 0 4px 24px rgba(0,0,0,0.12); border-radius: 12px; overflow: hidden; } .header-banner { background: linear-gradient(135deg, var(--c16-gold), var(--c16-pink), var(--c16-purple)); color: #fff; padding: 40px 48px; } .header-banner h1 { font-size: 2em; margin-bottom: 8px; font-weight: 700; } .header-banner .subtitle { font-size: 1.15em; opacity: 0.92; margin-bottom: 16px; } .header-banner .badge { display: inline-block; background: rgba(255,255,255,0.2); border: 1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.4); border-radius: 20px; padding: 4px 16px; font-size: 0.85em; margin-right: 8px; } .header-banner .badge-version { background: rgba(255,255,255,0.95); color: var(--c16-purple); font-weight: 700; } .meta-bar { background: #f8f9fa; border-bottom: 2px solid #e9ecef; padding: 16px 48px; display: flex; flex-wrap: wrap; gap: 24px; font-size: 0.9em; } .meta-item { display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 6px; } .meta-label { font-weight: 600; color: var(--c16-purple); } .content { padding: 32px 48px 48px; } .toc { background: #f8f9fa; border: 1px solid #e2e8f0; border-radius: 8px; padding: 24px 32px; margin-bottom: 36px; } .toc h3 { color: var(--c16-purple); margin-bottom: 12px; font-size: 1.1em; } .toc-grid { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr; gap: 6px 32px; } .toc a { color: var(--c16-dark); text-decoration: none; font-size: 0.92em; padding: 3px 0; display: block; } .toc a:hover { color: var(--c16-purple); } .section-title { color: var(--c16-purple); font-size: 1.45em; font-weight: 700; border-bottom: 3px solid var(--c16-pink); padding-bottom: 10px; margin: 40px 0 20px; display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 12px; } .section-num { background: var(--c16-pink); color: #fff; width: 32px; height: 32px; border-radius: 50%; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; font-size: 0.85em; font-weight: 700; flex-shrink: 0; } .sub-title { color: var(--c16-purple); font-size: 1.15em; font-weight: 600; margin: 28px 0 12px; } table { width: 100%; border-collapse: collapse; margin: 16px 0 24px; font-size: 0.9em; } thead th { background: var(--c16-purple); color: #fff; padding: 10px 14px; text-align: left; font-weight: 600; } tbody td { padding: 10px 14px; border-bottom: 1px solid #e2e8f0; vertical-align: top; } tbody tr:nth-child(even) { background: #f8fafc; } tbody tr:hover { background: #edf2f7; } .decision-box { background: linear-gradient(135deg, #f0fdf4, #ecfdf5); border: 2px solid var(--c16-green); border-radius: 8px; padding: 24px; margin: 24px 0; } .decision-box h4 { color: var(--c16-green); margin-bottom: 8px; } .warning-box { background: #fffbeb; border: 2px solid var(--c16-orange); border-radius: 8px; padding: 24px; margin: 24px 0; } .warning-box h4 { color: var(--c16-orange); margin-bottom: 8px; } .risk-box { background: #fef2f2; border: 2px solid var(--c16-red); border-radius: 8px; padding: 24px; margin: 24px 0; } .risk-box h4 { color: var(--c16-red); margin-bottom: 8px; } .info-box { background: #eff6ff; border: 2px solid var(--c16-blue); border-radius: 8px; padding: 24px; margin: 24px 0; } .info-box h4 { color: var(--c16-blue); margin-bottom: 8px; } .arch-diagram { background: #1a1a2e; color: #e0e0e0; padding: 24px; border-radius: 8px; font-family: 'Consolas', 'Courier New', monospace; font-size: 0.82em; line-height: 1.5; overflow-x: auto; white-space: pre; margin: 16px 0 24px; } .kpi-grid { display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(4, 1fr); gap: 16px; margin: 20px 0 28px; } .kpi-card { background: linear-gradient(135deg, #f8f9fa, #fff); border: 1px solid #e2e8f0; border-radius: 8px; padding: 16px; text-align: center; border-top: 3px solid var(--c16-purple); } .kpi-value { font-size: 1.8em; font-weight: 700; color: var(--c16-purple); } .kpi-label { font-size: 0.82em; color: #666; margin-top: 4px; } .badge-risk-high { background: var(--c16-red); color: #fff; padding: 2px 10px; border-radius: 12px; font-size: 0.82em; } .badge-risk-med { background: var(--c16-orange); color: #fff; padding: 2px 10px; border-radius: 12px; font-size: 0.82em; } .badge-risk-low { background: var(--c16-green); color: #fff; padding: 2px 10px; border-radius: 12px; font-size: 0.82em; } .badge-risk-none { background: #6b7280; color: #fff; padding: 2px 10px; border-radius: 12px; font-size: 0.82em; } .badge-status-done { background: var(--c16-green); color: #fff; padding: 2px 10px; border-radius: 12px; font-size: 0.82em; } .badge-status-partial { background: var(--c16-orange); color: #fff; padding: 2px 10px; border-radius: 12px; font-size: 0.82em; } .badge-status-todo { background: var(--c16-red); color: #fff; padding: 2px 10px; border-radius: 12px; font-size: 0.82em; } .badge-new { background: var(--c16-blue); color: #fff; padding: 2px 10px; border-radius: 12px; font-size: 0.82em; font-weight: 600; } .verdict { display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 8px; font-weight: 600; } .changelog { background: #eff6ff; border-left: 4px solid var(--c16-blue); padding: 16px 20px; margin: 16px 0; border-radius: 0 8px 8px 0; } .footer { background: #f8f9fa; border-top: 2px solid #e2e8f0; padding: 24px 48px; font-size: 0.82em; color: #666; } ul, ol { margin: 8px 0 16px 24px; } li { margin-bottom: 4px; } p { margin-bottom: 12px; } code { background: #f1f5f9; padding: 1px 6px; border-radius: 3px; font-family: 'Consolas', monospace; font-size: 0.9em; } strong { font-weight: 600; } @media print { .doc-wrapper { box-shadow: none; margin: 0; } .header-banner { -webkit-print-color-adjust: exact; print-color-adjust: exact; } }

![C16](data:image/jpeg;base64,)

# C16 Migration Decision Record

ADR-2026-001 — SAP API Policy Response: As-Is Gaps, Target Architecture & Migration Plan

v1.1 Architecture Decision Record INTERNAL — NOT FOR EXTERNAL DISTRIBUTION May 2026

Type: Architecture Decision Record

Status: DECIDED

Date: 2026-05-02

Classification: Internal

Version: 1.1 (added Source Control & Distribution)

**📋 v1.1 Changelog (2026-05-02):**

*   NEW Section 9: Source Control, Distribution & Version Control Architecture
*   NEW GitHub role in Connector distribution, ICF endpoint delivery, and abapGit compliance position
*   Updated To-Be architecture diagram (Section 6) to include GitHub/Container Registry
*   Updated Immediate Actions (Section 15) with source control tasks

### Table of Contents

[1\. Decision Trigger](#s1) [2\. As-Is Architecture](#s2) [3\. Gap Analysis (As-Is vs. Policy)](#s3) [4\. Options Considered](#s4) [5\. Decision](#s5) [6\. To-Be Target Architecture](#s6) [7\. Tool Migration Matrix (102 Tools)](#s7) [8\. C16 Connector Specification](#s8)

[9\. Source Control, Distribution & Version Control NEW](#s9) [10\. ICF Endpoint Status Register](#s10) [11\. Migration Timeline](#s11) [12\. Customer Deployment Tiers](#s12) [13\. Residual Risks After Full Migration](#s13) [14\. Document Impact Assessment](#s14) [15\. Immediate Actions (This Week)](#s15)

## 1 Decision Trigger

In April 2026, SAP issued a refreshed **SAP API Policy** that formalises a hard contractual line between Published APIs and everything else. Four specific provisions create compliance exposure for C16:

Clause

Verbatim Excerpt (from SAP API Policy PDF)

C16 Exposure

**§1.1 — Published APIs**

"APIs published on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub… or otherwise identified in product-specific Documentation ('Published APIs') are made available for the purposes described in that Documentation."

C16 uses ADT APIs which are **not** listed on the API Hub as Published APIs for customer/third-party consumption.

**§1.2 — Non-Published APIs**

"Customer and third-party applications must not access, invoke, or interact in any manner with APIs that are not Published APIs… **except as permitted by the Documentation or otherwise authorized by SAP (e.g., customers may use custom-developed ABAP interfaces in private cloud and on-premise deployments)**."

The named exception for custom-developed ABAP interfaces is C16's primary compliance path. C16's ICF endpoints (/sap/bc/zc16/\*) qualify under this exception.

**§3 — Anti-Circumvention**

"Customers, partners, and third parties must not bypass, disable, or otherwise circumvent API Controls, including through intermediary services, custom code or developments, proxies, gateways, impersonation techniques, or similar mechanisms."

C16 SaaS (Azure) currently acts as an intermediary between the user and SAP. The split-plane Connector architecture addresses this.

**api.sap.com ToU §3(m)**

"use any automated data gathering or extraction methods… (such as bots, scrapers, spiders, crawlers, **Model Context Protocol (MCP), agent-frameworks**, or any other text and data mining technology)… for artificial intelligence training, improvement or evaluation"

C16's `sap_api_hub_search` tool makes automated calls to api.sap.com. This is a separate legal instrument from the API Policy but creates additional exposure.

#### ⚠️ Important Interpretive Note

The official SAP API Policy PDF does **not** contain explicit, named anti-AI-agent language. References to "Section 2.2.2" in third-party reporting could not be verified against the published text. The AI implications are interpretive consequences of §1.2 (non-published API prohibition), §2 (bulk extraction controls), and §3 (anti-circumvention) read together, reinforced by SAP leadership statements distinguishing customer data from SAP's "domain knowhow / IP."

**Source:** SAP API Policy PDF verified 2026-05-01 at [https://help.sap.com/doc/sap-api-policy/latest/en-US/API\_Policy\_latest.pdf](https://help.sap.com/doc/sap-api-policy/latest/en-US/API_Policy_latest.pdf)

## 2 As-Is Architecture

102

Total Tools

29

ADT-Backed (Must Migrate)

17

RFC-Backed (Lower Priority)

6

OData (Compliant)

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ C16 SaaS (Azure) │ │ │ │ ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────────────┐ │ │ │ Claude LLM │ │ Python │ │ Tool Dispatcher │ │ │ │ (AI Engine) │ │ Engines │ │ (102 tools) │ │ │ │ │→ │ • Clean Core │→ │ • 29 ADT tools 🔴 │ │ │ │ Plans & │ │ • SOD │ │ • 17 RFC tools 🟡 │ │ │ │ selects │ │ • Perf │ │ • 6 OData tools 🟢 │ │ │ │ sequences │ │ • Fit2Std │ │ • 17 composite 🟡 │ │ │ └──────────────┘ └──────────────┘ │ • 1 api.sap.com 🔴 │ │ │ │ • 4 external 🟢 │ │ │ SAP Credentials: Azure Key Vault │ • 28 internal 🟢 │ │ │ (outside customer environment) 🔴 └──────────┬───────────┘ │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────┘ │ Site-to-Site VPN (IPSec) (not SAP-endorsed) │ ▼ ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Customer SAP System (On-Premise) │ │ │ │ ┌────────────────────────────┐ ┌────────────────────────────┐ │ │ │ /sap/bc/adt/\* (ADT APIs) │ │ /sap/bc/soap/rfc (RFC) │ │ │ │ NON-PUBLISHED 🔴 │ │ CLASSIC API (Level B) 🟡 │ │ │ └────────────────────────────┘ └────────────────────────────┘ │ │ ┌────────────────────────────┐ ┌────────────────────────────┐ │ │ │ /sap/bc/zc16/\* (ICF) │ │ /sap/opu/odata/\* (Gateway) │ │ │ │ CUSTOMER-OWNED ✅ │ │ PUBLISHED ✅ │ │ │ │ (7 of 13 migrated) │ │ │ │ │ └────────────────────────────┘ └────────────────────────────┘ │ └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

### Key Problems in As-Is

1.  **AI directly touches SAP** — the LLM (Claude) plans and executes API call sequences against the customer's SAP system. This matches the interpreted 2.2.2(a) pattern.
2.  **29 tools use ADT APIs** — ADT is not formally listed on api.sap.com as a Published API for customer/third-party consumption.
3.  **SAP credentials in Azure Key Vault** — credentials leave the customer's environment.
4.  **No SAP-endorsed architecture in path** — VPN is network infrastructure, not an SAP product.
5.  **api.sap.com dependency** — `sap_api_hub_search` makes automated calls to SAP's website.
6.  **Source code extraction** — full ABAP source transferred to C16 cloud for analysis.

## 3 Gap Analysis (As-Is vs. Policy)

Gap ID

Policy Clause

Current State

Required State

Severity

**G1**

§1.2 — Non-Published APIs

29 tools call ADT REST APIs directly

All SAP calls via customer-owned ICF endpoints or Published APIs

HIGH

**G2**

§3 — Anti-circumvention (interpreted)

AI (Claude) directly plans and executes API call sequences against SAP

AI never touches SAP — non-AI Connector executes calls inside customer environment

HIGH

**G3**

api.sap.com ToU §3(m)

`sap_api_hub_search` makes automated calls to api.sap.com

Remove dependency entirely — use C16 internal knowledge base

HIGH

**G4**

§2 — Bulk extraction controls

Source code extracted to C16 cloud for analysis. Code index built externally.

Analysis runs inside customer environment. Only results (scores, findings) sent to SaaS.

MEDIUM

**G5**

Data sovereignty (implied)

SAP credentials stored in Azure Key Vault (C16's subscription)

Credentials held exclusively in customer environment

MEDIUM

**G6**

§1.2 — "may change without notice"

ADT API dependencies can break on any SAP upgrade with no vendor accountability

Customer-owned ICF endpoints wrapping released ABAP classes (stable, versioned)

MEDIUM

**G7**

§3 — Monitoring and remedies

Heavy C16 workloads (indexing, bulk analysis) could trigger SAP throttling

Client-side rate limiting, incremental indexing, in-system processing

MEDIUM

## 4 Options Considered

Option

Description

2.2.2(a) Defense

§1.2 Defense

Creds in Customer?

Data in Customer?

Requires BTP?

Works for ECC?

Effort

Compliance

**A: Improve in Place**

Complete ICF migration, remove api.sap.com, add human approval gate, client-side rate limiting. Keep current SaaS→VPN→SAP architecture.

🟡 Human approval weakens "autonomous" characterization

⭐⭐⭐⭐ Customer-owned ICF endpoints

❌ No

❌ No

❌ No

✅ Yes

Low-Med

⭐⭐⭐ Defensible

**B: C16 Connector**

Split control plane / data plane. AI stays in Azure. Non-AI Connector runs in customer environment, makes all SAP calls. Only results sent back.

🟢 AI never touches SAP — Connector is not an AI system

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Customer's own infra calling customer's own APIs

✅ Yes

✅ Yes (raw data stays)

❌ No

✅ Yes

Medium

⭐⭐⭐⭐ Strong

**C: Full C16 in Customer**

Deploy entire C16 platform inside customer network. LLM via customer's own Azure OpenAI or local model.

🟢 Customer's own tool on customer's own infra

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Everything is customer's

✅ Yes

✅ Yes (100%)

❌ No

✅ Yes

High

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Maximum

**D: Cloud Connector + BTP**

Route C16 through SAP Cloud Connector via BTP Destination. Add to Option A or B.

🟢 SAP-endorsed architecture — triggers the policy exception directly

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ SAP-endorsed + customer-owned

🟡 In BTP Destination Service

❌ Data still flows to C16

✅ Yes

❌ No

Medium

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Maximum

## 5 Decision

#### ✅ DECIDED: Option A (Bridge) + Option B (Destination)

**Option A is the bridge.** Complete the ICF migration, remove api.sap.com, add human approval — these are immediate risk-reduction actions that can ship in Q2-Q3 2026 with minimal architecture change.

**Option B is the destination.** Build the C16 Connector as the strategic target architecture. AI never touches SAP. Credentials stay in customer environment. Analysis runs in-system. This is the structural fix.

**Option D is an optional enhancement.** For customers with BTP, add Cloud Connector on top of Option B for maximum compliance. Not required but recommended for RISE customers and regulated industries.

**Option C is reserved** for regulated industries (banking, defense, government) and air-gapped environments where no data can leave the customer network under any circumstances.

Decision Rationale

Option A Only

Option B Only

Option A + B (Chosen)

Time to first compliance improvement

🟢 Weeks (api.sap.com removal is immediate)

🔴 Months (Connector must be built)

🟢 **Weeks — Option A ships now**

2.2.2(a) AI agent defense

🟡 Weak — AI still touches SAP

🟢 Strong — AI never touches SAP

🟢 **Strong — Option B is the target**

Customer adoption

🟢 Zero change for customers

🟡 Requires customer VM

🟢 **Gradual — Option A first, B when ready**

Engineering effort

🟢 Low — mostly finishing in-progress work

🟡 Medium — new component to build

🟡 **Medium — parallel tracks**

Regulatory readiness

🟡 Defensible for most customers

🟢 Strong for all customers

🟢 **Progressive improvement**

## 6 To-Be Target Architecture

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ GitHub / Container Registry │ │ │ │ ┌───────────────────────────┐ ┌────────────────────────────────────┐ │ │ │ c16-platform (private) │ │ c16-connector (private) │ │ │ │ └─ CI/CD → Azure │ │ └─ CI/CD → Container Registry │ │ │ │ Container Apps │ │ (GHCR / ACR / Docker Hub) │ │ │ └───────────────────────────┘ └────────────────────────────────────┘ │ │ ┌───────────────────────────┐ ┌────────────────────────────────────┐ │ │ │ c16-icf-endpoints │ │ c16-skills (private) │ │ │ │ (customer-visible source) │ │ └─ 48 skill .md files │ │ │ │ └─ ABAP source for │ └────────────────────────────────────┘ │ │ │ customer installation │ │ │ └───────────────────────────┘ │ └────────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘ │ CI/CD (automated) ▼ ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ C16 SaaS (Azure) │ │ │ │ ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ │ │ Claude LLM │ │ SaaS Services: │ │ │ │ (AI Engine) │ │ • UI / Project Management │ │ │ │ │ │ • Document Generation │ │ │ │ Plans & │ │ • Skill Library │ │ │ │ generates │ │ • Customer Billing │ │ │ │ instructions │ │ • Connector Orchestration │ │ │ └──────┬───────┘ └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │ │ │ │ │ ██████████████████████████████████████████████████████████████████████ │ │ █ 🟢 AI NEVER TOUCHES SAP — ONLY SENDS SIGNED INSTRUCTIONS █ │ │ ██████████████████████████████████████████████████████████████████████ │ │ │ │ └─────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │ HTTPS (outbound from customer) │ Signed instructions (JWT/HMAC) ↓ │ Analysis results (JSON) ↑ │ │ NO SAP credentials │ NO raw source code │ NO bulk table data ▼ ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Customer Environment (VM / K8s / Cloud Container) │ │ │ │ ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ │ │ C16 Connector (Docker: c16/connector:latest) │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ ┌────────────────┐ ┌────────────────┐ ┌──────────────────┐ │ │ │ │ │ server.py │ │ Analysis │ │ SAP Client │ │ │ │ │ │ (instruction │ │ Engines: │ │ Libraries: │ │ │ │ │ │ receiver & │→ │ • Clean Core │→ │ • RFC/SOAP │ │ │ │ │ │ executor) │ │ • SOD │ │ • ICF/HTTPS │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ • Performance │ │ • OData V2/V4 │ │ │ │ │ │ JWT/HMAC │ │ • Fit2Std │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ validation │ │ • Code Metrics │ │ Calls ONLY: │ │ │ │ │ └────────────────┘ └────────────────┘ │ /sap/bc/zc16/\* │ │ │ │ │ │ /sap/opu/odata/\* │ │ │ │ │ ┌────────────────┐ ┌────────────────┐ └──────────────────┘ │ │ │ │ │ Credential │ │ Audit Log │ │ │ │ │ │ Vault: │ │ (local): │ NOT an AI system. │ │ │ │ │ AES-256-GCM │ │ Every SAP call │ Deterministic executor │ │ │ │ │ encrypted │ │ logged with │ of signed instructions.│ │ │ │ │ NEVER leaves │ │ correlation ID │ │ │ │ │ └────────────────┘ └────────────────┘ │ │ │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────┘ │ │ │ │ │ LAN (no internet needed) │ │ │ │ │ ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────▼───────────────────┐ │ │ │ Customer SAP System (ECC / S/4HANA) │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ ┌─────────────────────────────┐ ┌──────────────────────────┐ │ │ │ │ │ /sap/bc/zc16/\* (Customer │ │ /sap/opu/odata/\* │ │ │ │ │ │ ICF Endpoints) │ │ (Published OData) │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ ZCL\_C16\_REST\_BASE │ │ Standard Gateway services │ │ │ │ │ │ ZCL\_C16\_AUTH\_CHECK │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ ZCL\_C16\_AUDIT → API\_LOG │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ Wraps ONLY released ABAP │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ (verified via cloudification│ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ catalog each quarter) │ │ │ │ │ │ │ └─────────────────────────────┘ └──────────────────────────┘ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ ┌─────────────────────────────┐ │ │ │ │ │ abapGit classes │ ← From github.com/abapGit │ │ │ │ │ (SAP-recognized, community │ (open source, SAP-backed) │ │ │ │ │ standard for ABAP VCS) │ │ │ │ │ └─────────────────────────────┘ │ │ │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │ │ │ │ Optional: SAP Cloud Connector (for BTP customers) │ │ ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ │ │ Cloud Connector → BTP Destination Service │ │ │ │ (adds SAP-endorsed connectivity layer if customer has BTP) │ │ │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │ └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

### Key Architectural Principles

1.  **AI never touches SAP.** The LLM (Claude) plans analysis workflows and generates signed instructions. It never executes an API call against any SAP system.
2.  **The Connector is NOT an AI system.** It is a deterministic execution engine — same architectural pattern as SAP Cloud Connector, GitHub self-hosted runners, Azure Arc agents, Datadog agents.
3.  **Customer owns everything SAP-side.** ICF endpoints are Z-namespace, customer-transported, customer-authorized. Credentials never leave customer environment.
4.  **Released ABAP only.** ICF endpoints wrap only cloudification-verified released SAP classes. Verified quarterly.
5.  **Full audit trail.** Every SAP call logged in ZC16\_API\_LOG (customer-side) and Connector local audit log.
6.  **Source code managed via GitHub.** C16 platform, Connector images, ICF endpoint ABAP, and skills all version-controlled. Customer's generated code optionally version-controlled via abapGit.

ADT + abapGit

`/sap/bc/zc16/abapgit`

✅ Done

17

`sap_publish_service_binding`

Publish SRVB

ADT SB API

`/sap/bc/zc16/sb_publish`

✅ Done

18

`sap_batch_deploy_cds`

ADT DDL API

Not Started

`/sap/bc/zc16/abapgit`

✅ Done

`/sap/bc/zc16/sys_info`

✅ Done

`sap_create_transport`

29

`export_abap_sources`

ADT Source (bulk)

`/sap/bc/zc16/obj_search` (in-system search replaces bulk export)

Not Started

**Summary:** 9 done, 8 partial, 12 not started. Target: all 29 migrated by Q3 2026.

Tool

Function

Risk

Target

Tool

Function

Status

COMPLIANT

`sap_check_bsp_access`

### 7.4 — Composite Business Tools (17 tools — 🟡 Inherit Parent Risk)

Tools 53-69: `sap_open_po_report`, `sap_procurement_cycle_analysis`, `sap_po_detail_report`, `sap_vendor_po_summary`, `sap_material_po_summary`, `sap_purchase_reference_data`, `sap_inventory_status`, `sap_o2c_process_report`, `sap_o2c_audit_report`, `sap_discover_programs`, `sap_list_programs`, `sap_test_connection`, `sap_refresh_cache`, `sap_analyze_for_migration`, `sap_generate_rap_migration`, `sap_convert_smartform_to_adobe`, `sap_convert_smartform_to_adobe_full`. These are wrappers built on RFC/OData — risk inherits from underlying transport. All will route through the Connector in the target state.

### 7.5 — api.sap.com Tool (1 tool — 🔴 REMOVE)

#

Tool

Function

Action

Timeline

70

`sap_api_hub_search`

Search api.sap.com for APIs

**REMOVE ENTIRELY** — replace with C16 internal knowledge base

THIS WEEK

### 7.6 — External Non-SAP Tools (4 tools — 🟢 No Impact)

Tools 71-74: `deep_research`, `sap_deprecated_fm_lookup`, `clean_core_lookup`, `component_impact_check`. These connect to C16's internal databases or public web sources (not SAP). No policy impact.

### 7.7 — Internal C16 Tools (28 tools — 🟢 Never Touch SAP)

Tools 75-102: Analysis engines (`analyze_clean_core`, `analyze_performance`, `analyze_fit_to_standard`, etc.), document generation (`write_document`), workspace management (`container_write_file`, `container_edit_file`, etc.), skill management (`learn_skill`, `read_skill_file`), preview tools (`start_preview`, `stop_preview`), CAP tools (`init_cap_project`, `cap_validate_tool`, etc.), browser automation (`execute_browser_action`), shell (`shell_tool_fn`), deploy (`cf_deploy_tool`). These run locally on C16's platform and never interact with any SAP system.

## 8 C16 Connector Specification

### 8.1 — Components

c16-connector Docker image (~500MB) ├── server.py # Instruction receiver + executor ├── sap\_client/ # SAP API client libraries │ ├── rfc\_client.py # RFC via SOAP │ ├── icf\_client.py # Custom ICF endpoint calls │ └── odata\_client.py # OData V2/V4 ├── engines/ # Analysis engines (run locally) │ ├── clean\_core\_engine.py # CC1-CC9 analysis │ ├── sod\_engine.py # SOD conflict detection │ ├── code\_metrics.py # Source code metrics │ ├── performance\_engine.py # P1-P5 performance analysis │ └── fit\_to\_standard.py # Fit-to-standard assessment ├── search/ # Code search (runs locally) │ └── zoekt/ # Trigram search index ├── credentials/ # Encrypted SAP credentials │ └── vault.enc # AES-256-GCM encrypted ├── audit/ # Local audit log │ └── c16\_audit.log # Every SAP call logged └── config/ └── connector.yaml # SAP host, ports, TLS settings

### 8.2 — Data Flow

Direction

What Flows

What Does NOT Flow

**C16 SaaS → Connector**

Signed instructions (JWT/HMAC): "analyze program X", "read table Y where Z", "deploy object W"

SAP credentials, direct API parameters, raw connection strings

**Connector → C16 SaaS**

Analysis results (scores, findings, summaries), formatted data for report generation, deployment status

Raw source code, bulk table dumps, SAP credentials, PII

**Connector → Customer SAP**

ICF endpoint calls (/sap/bc/zc16/\*), OData calls (/sap/opu/odata/\*)

ADT API calls (eliminated), api.sap.com calls (eliminated)

### 8.3 — Infrastructure Requirements

Resource

Minimum

Recommended

OS

Any Linux with Docker

Ubuntu 22.04 LTS / RHEL 8+

CPU

2 vCPU

4 vCPU

RAM

4 GB

8 GB

Disk

20 GB

50 GB (with code search index)

Network to SAP

LAN or VPN

LAN (sub-ms latency)

Network to C16 SaaS

Outbound HTTPS (port 443)

Same

Docker Engine

20.10+

Latest stable

## 9 Source Control, Distribution & Version Control NEW in v1.1

GitHub serves as the version control, CI/CD, and distribution backbone for the entire C16 platform. This section documents every repository, its purpose, visibility, and how it relates to the compliance architecture.

### 9.1 — Repository Architecture

Repository

Visibility

Contents

CI/CD Target

Who Consumes

**c16-platform**

🔒 Private

C16 SaaS application — API server, tool dispatcher, prompt orchestration, UI

GitHub Actions → Azure Container Apps

C16 team only

**c16-connector**

🔒 Private

Connector Docker image — server.py, analysis engines, SAP client libraries

GitHub Actions → Container Registry (GHCR / ACR / Docker Hub)

Customer IT team (pulls Docker image)

**c16-icf-endpoints**

🔓 Customer-Visible \*

All ABAP source code for /sap/bc/zc16/\* endpoints — ZCL\_C16\_REST\_BASE, ZCL\_C16\_AUTH\_CHECK, all handler classes, installation scripts

No CI/CD — customer manually installs in their SAP system via transport

Customer ABAP team (reviews, installs, transports)

**c16-skills**

🔒 Private

48 skill .md files that guide C16's behavior and decision-making

Bundled into c16-platform image at build time

C16 team only

_\* "Customer-Visible" means the source code is provided to each customer as part of the onboarding package. This is NOT a public GitHub repository — it is shared with licensed customers under NDA. This deliberate transparency strengthens the §1.2 "customer-developed" argument because the customer can review, audit, and modify the ABAP code before installing it._

### 9.2 — abapGit: Role in the Architecture

#### ℹ️ What is abapGit?

[abapGit](https://github.com/abapGit/abapGit) is an open-source Git client for ABAP, hosted on GitHub. It is the de facto standard for ABAP version control, with active contributions from SAP employees. SAP recognizes abapGit as a community tool and promotes its use for custom ABAP development.

abapGit serves two roles in C16's architecture:

Role

How It's Used

Policy Assessment

**1\. Deployment Mechanism**

C16's `sync_abap_git_objects` tool calls the customer-installed `ZCL_C16_ABAPGIT` class, which uses abapGit's serialization/deserialization classes to create, update, and activate ABAP objects. This is the same mechanism used by abapGit's own UI and by SAP's gCTS.

LOW RISK — abapGit runs INSIDE the customer's SAP system. It is customer-installed, customer-owned code. SAP actively promotes abapGit. The underlying classes are well-established in the SAP ecosystem.

**2\. Customer Code Version Control (Future)**

In the Connector architecture, customer-generated ABAP code (reports, classes, CDS views) can optionally be version-controlled in the customer's own Git repository via abapGit. This creates a full audit trail of every code change C16 makes.

LOW RISK — Customer's own repo, customer's own review process. Strengthens the "customer-developed" argument. abapGit does not interact with api.sap.com or any SAP cloud service.

#### ✅ abapGit Compliance Position

abapGit is an **argument in C16's favor**, not a risk. If SAP challenged C16's use of abapGit, they would be challenging their own community ecosystem. abapGit is:

*   Open-source with SAP employee contributors
*   Installed on the customer's own system as customer code
*   Used by thousands of SAP customers and partners globally
*   The foundation of SAP's own gCTS (Git-enabled Change & Transport System)

### 9.3 — Connector Distribution Lifecycle

Step

Actor

Action

Artifact

**1\. Build**

C16 team

Merges code to `c16-connector` main branch

Git commit + tag (e.g., `v1.3.0`)

**2\. CI/CD**

GitHub Actions

Builds Docker image, runs tests, pushes to registry

`c16/connector:v1.3.0` in Container Registry

**3\. Notify**

C16 platform

Notifies customer's Connector that a new version is available

Update notification via C16 SaaS

**4\. Review**

Customer IT

Reviews changelog, decides whether to update

Changelog document

**5\. Pull**

Customer IT

`docker pull c16/connector:v1.3.0`

Updated Docker image on customer's VM

**6\. Restart**

Customer IT

`c16-connector restart`

New version running

**7\. Verify**

Customer IT

`c16-connector test`

Connectivity + version confirmation

**Key principle:** The customer controls the update cycle. C16 publishes new versions; the customer decides when (and whether) to pull them. This is the same model used by SAP Cloud Connector, Datadog Agent, and Azure Arc Agent.

### 9.4 — ICF Endpoint Source Code Delivery

Step

Actor

Action

Artifact

**1\. Develop**

C16 team

Writes/updates ABAP source for ICF endpoints

ABAP source in `c16-icf-endpoints` repo

**2\. Package**

C16 team

Creates versioned release with changelog and installation guide

ZIP / abapGit repo link shared with customer

**3\. Review**

Customer ABAP team

Reviews ABAP source code — line by line if desired

Customer's review notes / approval

**4\. Install**

Customer ABAP team

Imports via abapGit or manual copy into SE80/ADT. Creates transport.

Objects in customer's Z-namespace, in customer's transport

**5\. Test**

Customer ABAP team

Runs unit tests, verifies ICF endpoints respond correctly

Test results

**6\. Promote**

Customer Basis team

Transports through CTS: DEV → QA → PROD

Transport request released through each system

#### ✅ Why This Strengthens §1.2 Compliance

By providing ABAP source code to the customer (not compiled binaries), the customer:

*   **Reviews** every line before installing — they know exactly what runs on their system
*   **Compiles** the code in their own system — it becomes their binary
*   **Transports** through their own CTS — it follows their change management
*   **Owns** the installed objects — Z-namespace, customer package, customer transport
*   **Can modify** if needed — they have the source, not a black box

This makes the "customer-developed ABAP interface" characterization **unambiguous**. The customer reviewed it, compiled it, transported it, and operates it. It is their code.

### 9.5 — Version Control Strategy for Customer Code

ABAP code generated by C16 (reports, classes, CDS views) for the customer can optionally be version-controlled:

Approach

How It Works

Version History

Code Review

Rollback

Recommended For

**No Git (current default)**

C16 generates → deploys to SAP → done

❌ None (only SAP transport log)

❌ None

⚠️ Manual (reimport old transport)

Quick prototyping, $TMP development

**Customer's Own Git + abapGit**

C16 generates → customer's Git repo → PR review → abapGit pulls to SAP

✅ Full (Git commits)

✅ PR workflow

✅ Git revert + abapGit pull

Production deployments, enterprises with Git infrastructure

**C16-Managed Git (future option)**

C16 generates → per-customer Git repo on C16's GitHub org → abapGit pulls to SAP

✅ Full (Git commits)

🟡 C16 manages

✅ Git revert

Customers without own Git infrastructure

#### ⚠️ Recommendation: Customer's Own Git

For maximum compliance, customer-generated code should be in the **customer's own Git repository**, not C16's. This ensures the customer maintains full ownership of all code artifacts, with an independent audit trail that is not controlled by C16.

### 9.6 — GitHub Policy Impact Assessment

Question

Answer

Rationale

Does GitHub interact with SAP?

🟢 **No**

GitHub stores source code and builds images. It never makes API calls to any SAP system.

Does the SAP API Policy govern GitHub?

🟢 **No**

The policy governs how SAP APIs are called, not where code is stored.

Does abapGit create policy risk?

🟢 **No — it strengthens compliance**

abapGit is SAP-recognized, runs inside the customer's system, and is the foundation of SAP's own gCTS.

Should ICF endpoint source be customer-visible?

🟢 **Yes — strongly recommended**

Customer visibility strengthens the §1.2 "customer-developed" argument. The customer reviews, compiles, and transports the code.

Should customer-generated code be in Git?

🟢 **Yes — recommended (customer's own repo)**

Full audit trail, code review capability, rollback. Strengthens governance posture.

Endpoint

Path

Replaces (ADT)

Auth Objects

Status

In Progress

**System Info**

`/sap/bc/zc16/sys_info`

CL\_COS\_SYSTEM

None (read-only)

In Progress

`/sap/bc/zc16/goto_def`

CL\_WB\_OBJECT\_OPERATOR

S\_DEVELOP

Not Started

**ATC Run**

`/sap/bc/zc16/atc_run`

CL\_CI\_INSPECTION, CL\_CI\_CHECK

S\_DEVELOP

Not Started

**abapGit Sync**

`/sap/bc/zc16/abapgit`

ADT abapGit path

abapGit serializer/deserializer classes

S\_DEVELOP, S\_TRANSPRT

✅ Done (re-root path)

**Summary:** 8 done (including abapGit re-root), 3 in progress, 2 not started. Target: all 13 complete by Q3 2026.

## 11 Migration Timeline

Phase

Timeline

Deliverables

Compliance Impact

**Phase 0: Immediate**

This week (May 2026)

• Remove `sap_api_hub_search` entirely  
• Add mandatory human approval gate for multi-step workflows  
• Set up `c16-icf-endpoints` repo for customer source delivery  
• Document abapGit role in compliance position

Eliminates api.sap.com ToU violation. Weakens "autonomous" characterization.

**Phase 1: ICF Completion**

Q2 2026 (May-Jun)

• Complete obj\_search, obj\_source, sys\_info ICF endpoints  
• Validate all 10 migrated endpoints side-by-side with ADT  
• Move ICF endpoints out of $TMP into customer transport package  
• Version-tag ICF endpoint source in GitHub repo

Reduces ADT dependency from 29 tools to ~12

**Phase 2: Remaining ICF**

Q2-Q3 2026 (Jun-Jul)

• Build goto\_def, atc\_run ICF endpoints  
• Build syntax\_check, cds\_validate ICF endpoints  
• Build bsp\_deploy, cleanup ICF endpoints  
• Implement client-side rate limiting

Eliminates all remaining ADT dependencies. Full ICF coverage.

**Phase 3: Connector Build**

Q3 2026 (Jul-Sep)

• Build Connector Docker image (server.py + engines + SAP clients)  
• Implement signed instruction protocol (JWT/HMAC)  
• Migrate analysis engines from SaaS to Connector  
• Build `c16-connector` GitHub repo with CI/CD to registry  
• Customer deployment guide and install scripts

AI no longer touches SAP. Credentials in customer env only.

**Phase 4: Deprecate ADT**

Q4 2026 (Oct-Dec)

• Disable ADT direct path for customers with full ICF set  
• Feature flag for transition period  
• Update C16 Internal Reference to reflect new architecture  
• Customer communication and migration support

Complete elimination of non-published API usage.

**Phase 5: Continuous**

Ongoing (2027+)

• Monitor SAP Security Patch Day for new enforcement  
• Quarterly re-verification of released ABAP class status  
• Track SAP API Policy updates  
• Connector version updates via GitHub/registry

Ongoing compliance assurance.

## 12 Customer Deployment Tiers

Customer Landscape

Phase 0-2 (Option A: Improve in Place)

Phase 3-4 (Option B: Connector)

Optional Enhancement (Option D)

Final Compliance

**ECC On-Premise**

VPN + ICF endpoints + human approval + rate limiting

Connector on customer VM + Docker

N/A — ECC has no BTP integration

⭐⭐⭐⭐ Strong (§1.2 defense)

**S/4HANA On-Premise**

VPN + ICF endpoints + human approval + rate limiting

Connector on customer VM + Docker

Cloud Connector (if customer has BTP)

⭐⭐⭐⭐ Strong / ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ with CC

**RISE with SAP (Private Cloud)**

VPN + ICF endpoints + human approval + rate limiting

Connector on customer's cloud VM (same VNet)

Cloud Connector + BTP Destination (recommended)

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Maximum

**Regulated Industries**  
(banking, pharma, defense)

VPN + ICF endpoints (minimum baseline)

Connector mandatory. Analysis engines run in-system.

Cloud Connector + BTP (strongly recommended)

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Maximum

**Government / Air-Gapped**

Not viable — data leaves customer env

Full C16 on customer infra (Option C) + local LLM

N/A — no internet connectivity

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Maximum (fully isolated)

## 13 Residual Risks After Full Migration

Residual Risk

Description

Severity

Mitigation

**R1: C16 is still a generative AI system**

The AI nature of C16 cannot change. If SAP explicitly names AI-agent use as prohibited (currently interpretive, not explicit in policy text), the Connector architecture is the defense.

MEDIUM

AI never touches SAP (Connector). Human-in-the-loop. Fixed workflows. Seek SAP partnership for explicit endorsement.

**R2: SAP updates API Policy further**

SAP may tighten restrictions, add explicit AI-agent language, or expand enforcement to on-premise systems.

MEDIUM

Monitor SAP policy updates. Connector architecture is forward-compatible with stricter policies. BTP/CC path available if needed.

**R3: Released ABAP classes get reclassified**

SAP could reclassify currently-released ABAP classes as internal/private, breaking ICF endpoints.

LOW

Quarterly re-verification against cloudification catalog. Build fallback implementations for critical endpoints.

**R4: LLM prompts contain SAP context**

Even with Connector, the LLM receives analysis results that contain SAP-related context (table names, program structures, findings).

LOW

Results are summaries, not raw data. No source code or business data in prompts. Customer Azure OpenAI option available for maximum isolation.

**R5: VPN path for non-BTP customers**

Customers without BTP cannot use Cloud Connector. VPN + Connector is strong but not SAP-endorsed.

LOW

§1.2 defense applies. Connector architecture ensures AI never touches SAP regardless of pipe. Customer API gateway option adds governance layer.

## 14 Document Impact Assessment

C16 generates four categories of documents. Each has different SAP API exposure:

Document Type

Example

SAP APIs Used

api.sap.com Used

As-Is Risk

After Migration

**Clean Core Analysis**

ZMM\_VENDOR\_CONSOLIDATED\_REPORT analysis

ADT (source retrieval) + RFC (field lookups) + api.sap.com (Fiori app search)

🔴 Yes

HIGH

LOW — ICF endpoints + api.sap.com removed + analysis in Connector

**SOD / Security Analysis**

PEOL ECC SOD Analysis

RFC (USR02, AGR\_USERS, AGR\_1251, AGR\_AGRS reads)

🟢 No

MEDIUM

LOW — ICF endpoints + SOD engine in Connector

**Process Blueprint**

Casagrand RA Bill Process

RFC (table structure verification — minimal)

🟢 No

LOW

LOW — minimal change needed

**Process Reference**

Direct Stock Procurement Reference

None — pure AI knowledge

🟢 No

NONE

NONE — no change

**Key finding:** Document quality does NOT degrade after migration. The ICF migration changes the transport layer (how C16 gets data), not the content (what C16 generates). Every document C16 produces today will look identical after migration.

## 15 Immediate Actions (This Week)

#

Action

Owner

Deadline

Blocks

**IA-1**

Remove `sap_api_hub_search` from C16 platform. Replace all references with C16 internal knowledge base lookups.

C16 Engineering

End of this week

Nothing — independent action

**IA-2**

Implement mandatory human approval gate: C16 shows analysis plan → user must confirm "proceed" before any multi-step SAP workflow executes.

C16 Engineering

End of next week

Nothing — UI change only

**IA-3**

Do NOT share the v1.0 SAP API Policy Compliance Brief externally. It was based on the api.sap.com ToU, not the actual API Policy. Requires full rewrite against verified policy text.

C16 Leadership

Immediate

Nothing

**IA-4**

Engage legal counsel specializing in SAP licensing to review the API Policy and validate C16's §1.2 compliance position.

C16 Leadership

Within 2 weeks

Budget approval

**IA-5**

Begin search for SAP Platform Engineer / Cloud Integration Architect to design and build the Connector. Profile: SAP ICF + Docker/K8s + Azure/AWS + Security.

C16 HR / Leadership

Post within 2 weeks

Job description + budget

**IA-6**

Set up `c16-icf-endpoints` repository. Version-tag current ABAP source. Prepare customer-visible delivery package with installation guide.

C16 Engineering

End of next week

Nothing — repository setup

**C16 Migration Decision Record — ADR-2026-001 v1.1**

Document type: Architecture Decision Record | Status: DECIDED | Classification: INTERNAL

This document captures the architectural decision to migrate C16 from direct ADT API consumption to a split-plane Connector architecture with customer-owned ICF endpoints, in response to the SAP API Policy update of April 2026.

**Policy sources verified:** SAP API Policy PDF (help.sap.com, retrieved 2026-05-01), SAP Business Accelerator Hub Terms of Use (api.sap.com, last updated 2025-10-08), SAP Notes 3255746 and 3439624 (me.sap.com, retrieved 2026-05-01).

**Honesty note:** The official SAP API Policy does not contain explicit anti-AI-agent language. AI implications cited in this document are interpretive consequences of policy provisions read together with SAP leadership statements. This distinction is maintained throughout the document.

**Version history:** v1.0 (2026-05-02) — Initial decision record. v1.1 (2026-05-02) — Added Section 9 (Source Control, Distribution & Version Control), updated To-Be architecture diagram to include GitHub/Container Registry, added IA-6 (repository setup).

Generated by C16 Platform | 2026-05-02
